explanations.1: The sternal end is the triangular-shaped medial end of the clavicle that articulates with the manubrium of the sternum. The acromial end articulates laterally with the scapula.
explanations.2: A shoulder separation is an injury to the acromioclavicular joint, which connects the acromion of the scapula to the clavicle. The glenohumeral joint is the actual 'shoulder' joint, and its injury is a dislocation.
explanations.3: The supraspinous fossa is the narrow depression located superior to the spine of the scapula. The suprascapular notch is a landmark on the superior border itself, not a fossa.
explanations.4: The spine of the scapula is a prominent ridge that extends laterally to form the acromion, the bony tip of the shoulder. The coracoid process is a separate, anterior projection.
explanations.5: Each upper limb contains 30 bones (1 humerus, 1 radius, 1 ulna, 8 carpals, 5 metacarpals, and 14 phalanges). Combined, the two limbs contain 60 bones.
explanations.6: The greater tubercle is a large bony prominence located on the lateral side of the proximal humerus. The lesser tubercle is smaller and located on the anterior side.
explanations.7: The capitulum is a knob-like structure on the lateral distal humerus that articulates with the head of the radius. The trochlea articulates with the ulna.
explanations.8: In the proximal row of carpal bones, the scaphoid is the most lateral bone. The trapezium is also lateral but is located in the distal row.
explanations.9: The head of the radius articulates with the radial notch of the ulna at the proximal radioulnar joint. The radius is the lateral bone of the forearm, and its tuberosity is proximal.
explanations.10: The adult hip bone is formed by the fusion of three separate bones: the ilium, ischium, and pubis. These bones fuse together during late adolescence.
explanations.11: The ilium is the fan-like, superior region that forms the largest part of the hip bone. The ischium and pubis form the posteroinferior and anteroinferior portions, respectively.
explanations.12: The ischial tuberosity is the large, roughened protuberance of the inferior ischium that supports the body's weight when sitting. The iliac crest is the superior margin of the pelvis.
explanations.13: The ischial spine is a pointed projection located between the large greater sciatic notch and the smaller lesser sciatic notch. It serves as an important landmark in the pelvis.
explanations.14: The lesser trochanter is a bony projection on the medial side of the proximal femur that serves as an attachment site for powerful muscles. The head and condyles are articular surfaces.
explanations.15: The medial and lateral condyles of the tibia articulate with the femoral condyles to form the knee joint. The fibula does not participate in the knee joint.
explanations.16: The talus is the most superior tarsal bone; its superior surface articulates with the tibia and fibula to form the ankle joint. The calcaneus is the heel bone.
explanations.17: The foot contains 26 bones in total: 7 tarsals, 5 metatarsals, and 14 phalanges. This mirrors the structure of the hand, which has 27 bones (one more carpal than tarsal).
explanations.18: During the seventh week of development, the limbs undergo a 90-degree rotation. The upper limbs rotate laterally and the lower limbs rotate medially.
explanations.19: The epiphyseal plate is a layer of hyaline cartilage where bone lengthening occurs through the addition of new cartilage and its subsequent replacement by bone. Ossification centers in the epiphyses are secondary, not primary.
explanations.20: The clavicle is unique among long bones because it develops via intramembranous ossification, not endochondral. It is also the first bone in the body to begin ossification.
